architecture.summary: The system trains sparse autoencoders on the middle layer residual stream of Claude 3 Sonnet to extract interpretable features. The autoencoder learns a dictionary of up to 34 million features that decompose the model's internal activations into sparse, interpretable components. Information flows from the frozen language model's residual stream through the trained sparse autoencoder to produce feature activations; these features are then used for steering experiments and causal analysis by injecting manipulated activations back into the model.
trained_components: sparse autoencoder (dictionary learning on residual stream)

biblio.title: Scaling Monosemanticity: Extracting Interpretable Features from Claude 3 Sonnet

claims.statement: Sparse autoencoders can extract interpretable features from Claude 3 Sonnet, a production-scale language model, addressing the open question of whether dictionary learning methods scale beyond small transformers.
Trained sparse autoencoders with up to 34 million features on the model's middle layer residual stream.
The resulting features are multilingual and multimodal (generalizing to images despite text-only training).

Features can be used to steer model behavior in ways consistent with their interpretations, including features corresponding to famous entities and locations, abstract concepts like sarcasm or errors in code.
Features relevant to ways in which language models might cause harm--including features representing deception, power-seeking, sycophancy, and bias--causally influence model outputs when manipulated.
Our suite of features is incomplete, and we lack rigorous methods for evaluating whether our features faithfully capture model computations.
